author: dgusakov
content: <@755590043632140352> Can you confirm that MEV-boost will be deprecated with Gloas and validators will be able to get bids from gossip (trustless) and directly from builders (trusted and trustless) but not from the relays?

author: potuz
content:
The MEV boost software yes, it doesn't make sense to support it. Getting bids from the relay is another thing though. Relays can exist and broadcast requests and act as an intermediary via the builder API. I expect these to exist. 

Finally I've seen some push to have blinded payload envelopes on the beacon API. While the reason for these blinded envelopes is to increase performance in the API, they could in principle reenable MeV boost support. Prysm will not support MEV boost. I am unaware if any client will support it

author: dgusakov
content: Maybe I'm missing something, but for me, MEV-boost and relays were parts of the same concept. How validators will work with relays without MEV boost and what will be missing compared to MEV-Boost in this case

author: dgusakov
content: Also, having relays in the same way we have now will mean that the bids will be visible to everyone, which you mentioned as not desired result and the motivation for builders to reveal bids only to the exact validator that will propose a block (maybe you mentioned it not here but in some other chat)

author: potuz
content:
One way for relays to work without MeV boost: 

The relay holds the auction, it only receives bids
Proposer contacts the relay, 
The relay informs the builder it won, adjusts the price of the bid (for example to have a second price auction) signs and returns the bid to the relay
The relay replies to the proposer as if it was a builder

author: potuz
content: I have not mentioned sealed bids as something not desired. Different participants in the auction want different things. Presumably for proposers the above scheme with visible bids is not beneficial, but it would be for builders

author: potuz
content:
The argument however is that if there's any venue in which the auction is carried with sealed bids then all builders will degenerate to that. The only way to keep an open bid auction on the relay is if there's no defection among the top builders 

But this is beyond your question on MEV boost

author: potuz
content: Nothing prevents relays from existing.

author: dgusakov
content: If MEV-boost is no longer supported, what will happen to validators currently using it? Will they just localbuild?

I think the trade-off between performance and added complexity is not justified, also if the object is not defined anywhere might be a good thing to avoid mevboost-like ideas

author: nflaig
content: imo if in gloas a large set of proposer uses trusted bids, it largely a failure of the fork, at least wrt to that, the pipelining is still great of course

author: tersec
content: The other circumstance local builds might be used is if the circuit breaker kicks in (like today with the builder API

author: potuz
content:
I do not see any problem at all with trusted bids. The problem with trusted bids is if they are forced to be used. The win is already if there are competitive bids out there that are trustless. This makes the auction less opaque and centralized. A Large staker like Lido forcing nodes to use trusted bids is a problem cause this dissentivizes trustless bids and enshrines just a few builders. But if OTOH they allow trustless bids, they will eventually exist at least over P2P and just their mere existence is both a good fallback and a bottom price for the trusted auction. 

Having a trust relationship with existing builders is good, it is not a bad thing, existing builders do pay their bids and it allows for more efficient capital support without stressing the CL queues. We should not dissincentivized the use of trusted bids. We should just instead make sure that we allow trustless bids to exist.

author: nflaig
content:
> I do not see any problem at all with trusted bids. 
no not a big deal if people really want to, I will still force operators to set `--allow-dangerous-trusted-payments` so they are aware that they can get rugged by the builder at any point and rely on trust while with gloas, we have a 0 trust alternative. but yeah pretty much agree with what you said

author: tersec
content: Yeah, so from a support perspective this is problematic indeed because one just sees a missed proposal, and it's needed a bit of investigation every time. Nimbus will likely discourage, at least, trusted payments for this reason

author: dgusakov
content: The issue with not using trusted bigs is the drag on APR and the potential loss of the high bids when the market is hot and trustless bids do not include it

author: dgusakov
content: I think it all depends on the adoption of the trustless payments bu builders and not validators

author: dgusakov
content: If we can be sure that the median trusted bid is comparible with the median tructless, for sure we should encourage operators to use trustless

author: nflaig
content: why? if majority of validators only accept trustless bids then builders are forced to provide them, otherwise they miss out on the majority of slots

author: dgusakov
content: Yep. That might be an option. Tho I don't think that large CEXes will support the idea. They will still look for the highest MEV

author: dgusakov
content: And if 30% or so do support trusted payments, there will be no incentive for the builders to go all trustless

author: nflaig
content: when was the last 400 ETH block? also we've made staked builders so seamless now, there should be close to 0 downside for builders to use trustless bids

author: nflaig
content: titan should b easily able to stake 400 ETH imo

author: nflaig
content: they have enough churn to justify it

author: nflaig
content: to add to that, you can top-up within 1 slot now and withdraw the ETH with just a ~6 hours delay now

author: potuz
content:
It's complicated, there are a few reasons that go against this:

1. Submitting a large trustless bid is dangerous for the builder: if for whatever reason the bid doesn't land they have to pay it full. If the market is efficient and they are taking a small cut out of the searchers and proposers, then the risk/return ratio for the builder does not justify sending trustless bids. 
2. If the market is hot and txs are in the public mempool, then yes, trustless bids will surely be comparable. But if most transactions are just on one or two builders, they have a large advantage and can cartelize and provide bids well below their price and just above the p2p bids, given that we'd expect a very large difference between these block prices. 

The only way to make the market a bit more fair is if more builders appear with access to more transactions as the mempool is dead.  Examples of builders that could exist are

1. Large applications with their own UI, doing their own sequencing and submitting over P2P. 
2. Rollup operators that have feed information
3. Cexes (presumably they already do this though).

If enough entities manage to get wallets to port transactions to them and we provide a safe simple way of building, then I'd expect bids over p2p to exist. A simple example would be flashbots trusting their feed to some respectable entities. For example Prysm being a large Lido operator. Flashbots could provide their feed to Prysm to sequence and bid over P2P, without making any profit (except the expected value to have E/V being zero instead of negative). This way the auction would be trully more effective.

author: nflaig
content: regarding (1), so with trusted bid you just rug pull the proposer that you promised to pay via the tx in the payload?

author: potuz
content: my understanding is that that is in fact the proposal, and that still the E/V because of the difference in bid prices is beneficial for large pools in the long run

author: nflaig
content: I think this is the opposite direction, as soon as there is a large enough set that **only** accepts trustless bids, then builders will be forced to provide them if they wanna compete for those slots, at that point, they will have to stake enough ETH anyways, so there is no downside for them

author: nflaig
content: also btw, I am not suggesting using p2p bids only, builder api is perfectly fine and will be the most profitable path, I am just saying use trustless payments

author: potuz
content: Take a look here, large blocks are a bit frequent. https://x.com/mevproposerbot Most now aren't even covered by this bot.

author: nflaig
content: yep already following that one, but haven't checked in a while

author: potuz
content: I understand, but like it or not, the trustless path has a cost over the trusted one, and at the very least this cost needs to creep out from somewhere. It does make the market less efficient. At any rate, the biggest generator of assymetry, by orders of magnitude, is neither of these things, and it's private order flows. We need to attack that problem, with applications and wallets, before trying to get operators to act irrationally and make less money

author: potuz
content: And honestly, I myself am hoping that we can get rid of both trusted and trustless payments and just go with randomly selected builders and MEV burn

author: dgusakov
content: So on that. I see no reasons for Lido do prohibit trustless bids.

author: potuz
content: that would be a big difference

author: nflaig
content: I talked to rocketpool before, they will allow it too from last time I checked

author: potuz
content: It's also hard to **not** allow it cause at least Prysm will not make it easy to disable p2p bids and it would be literally impossible to do it completely.

author: dgusakov

author: potuz
content: So if you use PRysm and the winning bid is trustless, we will not provide you a way to blacklist it

author: dgusakov
content: Great!

author: nflaig
content: still not understanding the cost really, besides having to lock up capital there shouldn't be any

author: potuz
content: Capital lockup and the expected failure to deliver from time to time are not negligible.

author: potuz
content: lockup it's not only that the capital is locked, but that it has to be managed

author: nflaig
content: I am arguing the capital lock up is negligible for large builders and to not pay from time to time and rug pull the proposer instead is just an argument why every proposer should use trustless

author: nflaig
content: agree, but at least we did everything from the protocol pov to make it as nice for them as possible

author: potuz
content: I think you're just guessing this without doing the numbers. For Potuz' validator of course it makes sense to use only trustless since I land a block every 3 months. For a staker as Lido that lands blocks every epoch, the rational thing to do is to optimize for the long term numbers, and if trusted bids promise to be larger and lose an eventual rug if the builder fails to provide the block for a bug in the builder, then they can definitely have this trust relationship and take that eventual failure as just a bug. The rational thing to do is to take trusted bids in this case.

author: potuz
content: if the alternative is to embue the cost of that failure on every single bid and send it trustless, everyone loses

author: potuz
content: My node of course will force trustless. At any rate, what I trully want to stress is that no one really wins by enforcing that trustless bids need to land on blocks. We win by enforcing that trustless bids need to exist, need to be delivered to proposers and need to be competitive with trusted ones

author: potuz
content: it doesn't matter if they never win, they just need to compete

bit skeptical about this, but we need actual mainnet data to find out, I think we are on the same page

author: yorickdowne
content:
<@755590043632140352> 
>The MEV boost software yes, it doesn't make sense to support it
>Prysm will not support MEV boost

Pointing out here that commit-boost wants to be a thing still, and Prysm has a PR to allow proxy (sidecar) and builder URL. <https://github.com/OffchainLabs/prysm/pull/17124> . This would support both mev-boost and commit-boost or any sidecar like it, as I read it.

I am still trying to wrap my head around ePBS and Glamsterdam. I have no opinion whether having `builders` for builder URLs as well as `proxy` for a side car is desirable or not.

author: nflaig
content:
> Prysm will not support MEV boost
I think he means the blinded flow, but in theory if mev boost is updated for gloas staked builder flow it can still be used, but it doesn't add any value on top of what clients will provide by default, so adding a middleware with 0 extra value doesn't make any sense. commit boost is a different story as it implements stuff on top of just the pbs pipeline

author: jvranek
content: from the commit boost side we're *only* looking at the gloas flow! and yes besides the pbs pipeline we're still focusing on proposer commitments. ideally with the gloas flow sidecars can continue to act as experimentation zones w/o requiring forked CLs + potentially absorb customer service headaches that come from handling operator feat reqs

author: dgusakov
content:
To keep it clear. Now we attach a validator to MEV-Boost, specify relays, and MEV-Boost talks to relays, right?

Given all said above, with Gloas we will simply put relays to VC directly as if they were builders and there is no need for MEV-Boos, right?

Sorry if my questions sound dumb to you, but I never went into internals of CL client or MEV-Boost... I just know the basics here

author: nflaig
content:
today the relays (essentially a set of urls) are configured on the mev-boost side, the validator client talks to the beacon node which just talks to a single endpoint on mev-boost, and mev-boost does the multiplexing/connections to relays

in gloas, the builders (or relays) are configured on the validator client side, which again talks to the beaocn node, but the beacon node no longer needs a sidecar, it directly talks to builders/relays, does the multiplexing, bid selection etc. that is handled by mev-boost today

<@755590043632140352> please confirm this, especially the post-gloas flow, I think you intend to have the whole config be done in the validator client, and beacon node just does whatever the vc wants

author: jvranek
content: this is how terrence's PR (https://github.com/OffchainLabs/prysm/pull/17124) works currently although it doesn't send `min_bid` or `builder_boost` which potuz's PR has
